narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 72% chance that you would not pass, based on patterns from thousands of past students' academic and background details. This high predicted risk of failure triggered the rejection to help the school maintain strong bar passage rates. The model's decision hinged most strongly on your Year 3 class rank, which fell in the second decile—far below the fifth or higher decile typical for students who went on to pass the bar—pushing the prediction heavily toward failure by signaling potential struggles in advanced coursework. Your Year 1 rank in the third decile also contributed notably in the same direction, as it was lower than the sixth decile average among bar passers, reinforcing concerns about early academic momentum. On a more positive note, your LSAT score of 30.5 worked in your favor, pulling the prediction somewhat toward passing since it showed solid reasoning ability, though not enough to offset the ranking issues. While your undergraduate GPA of 3.3 exceeds the 3.24 average for bar passers and your middle family income aligns with over a third of successful students, these did not carry enough weight here, nor did your choice to study full-time, which is common among passers. Compared to typical admitted students, who often enter with top-half rankings in both early and later years alongside higher LSATs around 37, your profile stood out as riskier in the areas the model prioritizes most. This isn't a judgment on your potential or background—gender and race weren't factors—but a data-driven call to protect the program's outcomes. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score, strengthening your later-year performance through targeted tutoring or additional undergrad courses, or exploring part-time programs or bridge courses that have helped similar students build stronger records. Many applicants in your position have succeeded by addressing these key gaps and reapplying with refreshed credentials—your solid GPA and full-time commitment give you a great foundation to build on. Keep pushing; persistence often turns predictions around.
